steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content:
Clause 1: "They are hardly pristine, though" — a complete sentence in which "though" functions as a sentence-final hedge attached to the first clause.

Clause 2: "many chondrites experience aqueous alteration as a result of exposure to fluids, as well as fracturing, veining, and localized melting due to collisions with other objects" — also a complete sentence.

Two independent clauses must be joined by appropriate punctuation, not a comma alone.

steps.title: Apply the rule and check each choice
steps.content:
The correct boundary between two independent clauses (with no coordinating conjunction) is a semicolon. "Though" here is a sentence-final adverb, set off by a comma at the end of clause 1; the semicolon then marks the clause boundary.
  • Choice A — "pristine, though": leaves only a comma between two independent clauses, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — "pristine, though;": comma sets off the sentence-final "though," and the semicolon properly joins the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice C — "pristine; though": places the semicolon before "though," so "though" would have to introduce clause 2 as a conjunctive adverb but is not followed by the required comma, and the orphaned "though" reads awkwardly. ✗
  • Choice D — "pristine, though,": surrounds "though" with commas as a parenthetical but supplies no semicolon, leaving the two independent clauses joined only by a comma. ✗
